questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a client's condition is deteriorating on the unit?
questions.scenario: A client becomes increasingly short of breath with new confusion and an oxygen saturation drop despite supplemental oxygen.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse has already repositioned the client and verified the pulse oximeter reading.

options.text: Activate the rapid response team and escalate immediately
Wait 30 minutes to see if the client improves
Ask the family to decide whether to call the provider
Document the change and continue routine rounding

questions.rationale: New confusion plus worsening respiratory status are red-flag instability cues. Management-of-care priorities include escalating early, activating rapid response resources, and preventing delay in rescue care.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Wait 30 minutes to see if the client improves, and misses that New confusion plus worsening respiratory status are red-flag instability cues. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Ask the family to decide whether to call the provider is that it waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, despite the clinical warning in A client becomes increasingly short of breath with new confusion and an oxygen saturation drop despite supplemental oxygen. The priority is Activate the rapid response team and escalate immediately, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Document the change and continue routine rounding would be unsafe, instead of responding to A client becomes increasingly short of breath with new confusion and an oxygen saturation drop despite supplemental oxygen. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: When instability cues appear, escalate early rather than waiting.
questions.speedCue: New confusion plus hypoxia is a rescue trigger.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a client taking warfarin shows bleeding risk and a rising INR?
questions.scenario: A client taking warfarin for atrial fibrillation reports black stools and bleeding gums.
questions.additionalInfo: Today's INR is markedly above therapeutic range and the next dose is due soon.

options.text: Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol
Give the next dose on time because stopping anticoagulation is always more dangerous
Encourage extra green vegetables right now instead of notifying the provider
Tell the client mild bleeding is expected and to call back next week if it worsens

questions.rationale: Bleeding symptoms with a supratherapeutic INR require immediate hold-and-escalate thinking, not routine dosing. The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Give the next dose on time because stopping anticoagulation is always more dangerous is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, even though the higher-risk finding is The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment. Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol is safer because it addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage extra green vegetables right now instead of notifying the provider fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Today's INR is markedly above therapeutic range and the next dose is due soon. Substitutes a nonurgent action for required clinical escalation, so The safer answer is Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol because delaying bleeding-risk intervention can worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Tell the client mild bleeding is expected and to call back next week if it worsens is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Bleeding plus high INR means pause the anticoagulant and escalate.
questions.speedCue: Black stools plus high INR means hold before you harm.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move with this back-pain presentation?

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this hemodynamic decline?

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers
The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false
The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction
The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

distractorRationales.c: The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction would not address the priority, and misses that Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so It is less defensible because This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.stem: A fire starts in a client room where oxygen is in use. Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority actions?
questions.scenario: Smoke is visible near a wastebasket while the client is receiving oxygen by nasal cannula.

options.text: Rescue the client and remove the oxygen source if safe, then activate alarm and contain fire per policy
Find the cause of the fire first before moving the client
Continue oxygen and wait for security to arrive
Open the door to improve ventilation and reduce smoke

questions.rationale: Fire response follows RACE (Rescue, Alarm, Contain, Extinguish/Evacuate). With oxygen present, removing the client and limiting fuel/oxygen if safe reduces fire spread risk while alarms and containment steps are activated.

distractorRationales.b: Find the cause of the fire first before moving the client would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by A fire starts in a client room where oxygen is in use. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Rescue the client and remove the oxygen source if safe, then activate alarm and contain fire better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Continue oxygen and wait for security to arrive, despite the clinical warning in Smoke is visible near a wastebasket while the client is receiving oxygen by nasal cannula. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Rescue the client and remove the oxygen source if safe, then activate alarm and contain fire because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Open the door to improve ventilation and reduce smoke fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in RACE comes first. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.

questions.takeaway: RACE comes first; rescue and alarm actions are immediate priorities.
questions.speedCue: In fires, rescue and alarm happen before investigation.

visualRationale.caption: RACE comes first; rescue and alarm actions are immediate priorities.
visualRationale.conclusion: RACE comes first; rescue and alarm actions are immediate priorities.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after an adolescent screens positive for depression?

Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority before this client receives IV contrast?

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after recognizing that the wrong dose of insulin was given?

Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization while unstable glucose physiology progresses.
distractorRationales.d: Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by the harmed or unstable client always comes first. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete because delaying glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization can worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Reporting matters, but the harmed or unstable client always comes first.
questions.speedCue: Error chain shortcut: patient first, provider second, paperwork third.

questions.stem: Which findings in this febrile client support the nurse's concern for meningococcal sepsis? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A college student arrives with fever, severe headache, malaise, and rapidly worsening illness.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding which additional findings would strengthen concern for a meningococcal emergency rather than a routine viral illness.

options.text: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash
Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion
Neck stiffness and photophobia
A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure
Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids
Acute confusion or worsening mental status

questions.rationale: Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis.

distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, even though the higher-risk finding is The fast clue is not fever alone. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.e: The problem with Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, rather than acting on fever plus rash and collapse signs. The priority is A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash plus Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion plus Neck stiffness, not a step that delays stabilization of infection progression.

questions.takeaway: The fast clue is not fever alone, but fever plus rash and collapse signs.
questions.speedCue: Nonblanching rash plus neuro change or shock is the shortcut.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which tasks are appropriate for the nurse to delegate to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) for a stable adult client? Select all that apply.

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs for a client with chronic heart failure
Teach a client how to use an incentive spirometer
Ambulate a postoperative day 2 client who has been cleared to walk
Assess a newly admitted client for chest pain
Measure and document intake and output for a stable client

questions.rationale: UAP can perform routine, non-assessment, non-teaching tasks for stable clients (vitals, ambulation per plan, I&O). Teaching and initial assessment require RN judgment.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Teach a client how to use an incentive spirometer would be unsafe, instead of responding to UAP can perform routine, non-assessment, non-teaching tasks for stable clients (vitals, ambulation per plan, I&O). Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Assess a newly admitted client for chest pain as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by UAP can perform routine, non-assessment, non-teaching tasks for stable clients (vitals, ambulation per plan, I&O). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Obtain routine vital signs for a client with chronic heart failure plus Ambulate a postoperative day; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Delegate stable routine tasks; keep assessment and teaching with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness, and left shoulder pain. Which findings support the nurse's concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy?
questions.scenario: The client looks pale and anxious in triage. Blood pressure is 86/48 mm Hg and heart rate is 128/min.

options.text: Hypotension
Shoulder pain
Rigid abdomen
Light pink spotting only
Tachycardia
Improving pelvic discomfort after rest

questions.rationale: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding. The nurse should connect these findings to hemorrhagic instability instead of treating them as routine first-trimester discomfort.

distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Rigid abdomen, despite the clinical warning in referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of bleeding or anticoagulation injury and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Light pink spotting only, while the decisive cue is shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Hypotension plus Shoulder pain plus Tachycardia, not a step that delays stabilization of bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.f: Improving pelvic discomfort after rest fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Hypotension plus Shoulder pain plus Tachycardia; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: When pregnancy, unilateral pain, shoulder pain, and shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast.
questions.speedCue: Pregnancy plus unilateral pain plus shoulder pain plus hypotension is a hemorrhage pattern, not a routine discomfort pattern.

questions.stem: Which findings support the nurse's concern that this newborn is becoming hypoglycemic? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period.

options.text: Jitteriness with tremulous extremities
Weak or poor feeding effort
Low axillary temperature
Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting
Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness

questions.rationale: Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding, and temperature instability. The nurse should cluster the subtle findings together quickly instead of waiting for a dramatic crash.

distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting, while the decisive cue is Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Choosing it can delay glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization and leave unstable glucose physiology unresolved.
distractorRationales.e: This option makes Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness the focus, without accounting for The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Jitteriness with tremulous extremities plus Weak or poor feeding effort plus Low axillary temperature better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
questions.speedCue: Jitteriness plus poor feeding should move glucose near the top immediately.

visualRationale.caption: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
visualRationale.conclusion: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for C. difficile. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The nurse receives the result just before transferring the client to a semi-private room.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit has gowns, gloves, soap-and-water handwashing sinks, and sporicidal disinfectant available.

options.text: Initiate contact-plus-enteric precautions with gown and gloves
Place the client in an appropriate room and dedicate equipment when possible
Perform soap-and-water hand hygiene after care
Ensure the environment is cleaned with a sporicidal product

questions.rationale: C. difficile prevention works as a bundle: start precautions, protect room placement and equipment, use soap-and-water hand hygiene, and ensure sporicidal cleaning. The sequence keeps spores from spreading while also protecting the next patient environment.
questions.takeaway: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is spores: alcohol gel alone is not enough.

visualRationale.title: C_diff_transmission_prevention_bundle
visualRationale.caption: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
visualRationale.conclusion: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.

questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best supports a central-line insertion bundle to reduce bloodstream infection risk.
questions.scenario: A provider is preparing to insert a central venous catheter at the bedside.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is responsible for bundle support and stopping the procedure if sterile breaks occur.

options.text: Perform hand hygiene and confirm the sterile setup is complete
Use maximal barrier precautions and proper skin antisepsis
Pause the procedure if sterile technique is broken
Document line necessity and bundle completion after insertion

questions.rationale: CLABSI prevention depends on hand hygiene, maximal barrier technique, chlorhexidine skin prep, speaking up about sterility breaks, and documenting bundle adherence. The nurse should not treat a sterile break as a minor issue when the line is still being placed.
questions.takeaway: Sterile discipline is part of the line procedure, not optional aftercare.
questions.speedCue: If the sterile field breaks, the next move is stop, not excuse.

questions.stem: For each task, click to specify whether the RN may delegate the task to an experienced UAP on a medical-surgical unit.
questions.scenario: The unit is short-staffed and the RN is managing several stable clients plus one new admission.

options.text: May delegate to UAP
Do not delegate to UAP

matrixRows.label: Obtain vital signs for a stable client
Teach a client to use a new device
Perform the initial assessment for a new admission
Ambulate a stable client with a gait belt

questions.rationale: Delegation focuses on stability and predictability. Routine tasks with predictable outcomes may be delegated to UAP; assessment, teaching, and evaluation remain RN responsibilities.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with May delegate to UAP is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, while the decisive cue is keep assessment, teaching. The nurse should prioritize the row-specific answer pattern; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.b: Selecting Do not delegate to UAP would be unsafe, rather than acting on Delegation focuses on stability and predictability. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize the row-specific answer pattern; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine stable-care tasks; keep assessment, teaching, and evaluation with the RN.
questions.speedCue: If the task requires judgment or evaluation, it stays with the RN.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent monitoring picture.
questions.scenario: The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening.
questions.additionalInfo: The provider asks the nurse to identify which findings point to deterioration that needs faster action.

options.text: Supports worsening acute kidney injury
Less urgent monitoring picture

matrixRows.label: Rising creatinine with falling urine output
Stable creatinine with adequate hourly urine output
Improved intake with steady daily weight

questions.rationale: Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in Supports worsening acute kidney injury is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for renal protection while renal injury progresses.
distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Less urgent monitoring picture is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for renal protection while renal injury progresses.

questions.takeaway: Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI.
questions.speedCue: Creatinine up and urine down means kidney injury is moving the wrong way.

questions.stem: An RN is working with an experienced UAP. Which task is appropriate for the RN to delegate to the UAP?

options.text: Obtain vital signs for a stable client 1 day after surgery
Perform the initial assessment for a newly admitted client
Teach a client how to use a new inhaler
Evaluate the effectiveness of a pain medication

questions.rationale: Tasks appropriate for delegation to UAP are routine, noninvasive, and have predictable outcomes. Initial assessments, teaching, and evaluation require RN judgment and cannot be delegated to UAP.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Perform the initial assessment for a newly admitted client is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that keep assessment, teaching. Obtain vital signs for a stable client 1 day after surgery is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Teach a client how to use a new inhaler, and misses that keep assessment, teaching. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so It is less defensible because Obtain vital signs for a stable client 1 day after surgery targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Evaluate the effectiveness of a pain medication as the key issue, while the decisive cue is Tasks appropriate for delegation to UAP are routine, noninvasive. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine, predictable tasks; keep assessment, teaching, and evaluation with the RN.
